title: Shrimp and grits
subtitle: Gulf prawns in a bacon and tomato sauce on creamy stone-ground grits
excerpt: The South Carolina Lowcountry classic: large prawns sautéed with smoked bacon, garlic, tomatoes, and a little hot sauce, served on a bowl of creamy, buttery polenta-style grits. One of the most elegant things you can put together in 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Shrimp and grits as a dish is documented in the South Carolina and Georgia Lowcountry from the nineteenth century, where shrimp were plentiful and grits (ground hominy) were a staple. The preparation gained wider national recognition following Craig Claiborne's 1985 article in the New York Times, which described the dish as served at Crook's Corner in Chapel Hill, North Carolina. It is now one of the most recognised dishes of American Southern cooking.

content.text: The grits are the base of the dish and deserve as much attention as the prawns. Stone-ground grits take 25 to 30 minutes to cook properly, the quick-cook variety produces a thinner, less interesting result. Cook them in a mix of milk and water with a good quantity of butter stirred in at the end; they should be thick enough to hold a well in the centre for the prawn topping, but creamy and flowing rather than stiff. Season generously with salt.

In the UK, polenta is the closest equivalent to stone-ground grits in texture and behaviour, and fine-ground polenta or coarse cornmeal both work well. The prawns themselves need only 2 to 3 minutes in the pan, large raw king prawns, pink all the way through and slightly curled, are done. Overcooked prawns are rubbery; remove them the moment the flesh is opaque.

items.name: Polenta
items.prepNote: stone-ground grits, fine polenta, or coarse cornmeal

content.text: Bring the {{whole-milk}} of milk and water to a simmer in the saucepan with a generous pinch of salt. Gradually whisk in the {{polenta}} of cornmeal or grits in a thin, steady stream to prevent lumps. Cook over low heat, stirring frequently, for 20 to 30 minutes until thick and creamy. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{cheddar}} of cheddar if using. Season generously with salt. Keep warm over the lowest heat.

Cook the {{streaky-bacon}} of bacon lardons in the frying pan over medium heat for 4 to 5 minutes until the fat has rendered and the edges are crisp. Add the {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and cook for 30 seconds. Add the {{tomato}} of diced tomato and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{prawns-raw}} of prawns in a single layer. Cook for 1 to 2 minutes per side until pink and just cooked through. Add the {{spring-onion}} of spring onions, {{louisiana-hot-sauce}} of hot sauce, and a squeeze of {{lemon}} of lemon juice. Toss briefly.
Spoon the grits into wide, warm bowls. Make a well in the centre and spoon the prawn and bacon mixture over. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Whisk constantly as you add the cornmeal in a very slow, thin stream; stir out any lumps as they form
items.cause: Cornmeal added too fast to the hot liquid
items.symptom: Grits are lumpy and not smooth

items.fix: Add a splash of warm milk or water and stir vigorously; grits need generous butter (or cream) to stay creamy rather than stiff
items.cause: Cooked too thick, or not enough fat stirred in at the end

items.fix: Cook until just opaque, 1 to 2 minutes per side for large prawns; they will continue to cook slightly in the residual heat after you remove them
items.cause: Overcooked, even 30 seconds too long in a hot pan makes prawns rubbery

items.fix: Add a splash of chicken stock or water to the pan after the prawns are done and swirl to loosen the fond into a light sauce
items.cause: Tomatoes did not release enough juice, or bacon fat absorbed during cooking
items.symptom: Sauce is too dry and the prawns look dull

content.text: Shrimp and grits is a dish of the South Carolina and Georgia Lowcountry. The tidal marshes and estuaries there have supplied shrimp to local cooking for centuries. Hominy grits, made from dried corn treated with lye, have been a staple since Native American times. For much of its history the dish was a fishermen's breakfast, eaten before the boats went out. It rose to national restaurant fame through the Chapel Hill restaurant Crook's Corner in the 1980s, and is now one of the dishes that defines the American South in restaurants from New Orleans to New York.
